file_name: Vivekananda et al. - 2023 - Retracing-efficient IoT model for identifying the skin-related tags using automatic lumen detection.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly states that a 'Boltzmann belief network deep learning technique' was used to train data for artificial intelligence (AI) to forecast diseases. Additionally, it mentions using a 'Random Forest (RF) algorithm' for forecasting problems and classifying skin tags, moles, and warts. The study also references the use of 'spatial transcriptomics' and 'GWAS' for prediction, alongside IoT-based monitoring for identifying skin-related tags.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel 'Retracing-efficient IoT model' for identifying skin-related tags using automatic lumen detection. It details specific methods including IoT-based Variation regularity, trigonometric algorithms, Sine Cosine Algorithm (SCA), and Random Forest (RF) classification. The study presents original experimental results, such as accuracy metrics (e.g., 95.9% enhanced low-level total sample accuracy, 87% periodic classification accuracy) on datasets of 500 to 10000 images, and includes a literature review table but primarily focuses on presenting new technical contributions and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Retracing-efficient IoT model for identifying the skin-related tags using automatic lumen detection
brief_summary: This study proposes a Retracing-efficient IoT model to identify moles, skin tags, and warts using automatic lumen detection and IoT-based variation regularity. The system integrates a trigonometric algorithm, spatial transcriptomics, and a Random Forest classifier to analyze skin images and predict dermatological conditions. Evaluation was performed on datasets containing 500 to 10,000 images with resolutions ranging from 480 × 640 to 2336 × 3504 pixels. Key results included a heightened intensity accuracy of 56.2%, an enhanced low-level total sample accuracy of 95.9%, and a periodic classification accuracy of 87%. The authors conclude that the proposed IoMT-based surveillance system offers a robust, low-cost, and effective platform for the early detection and diagnosis of skin diseases.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study identifies moles as a primary target. The paper defines moles as nevi, listing subtypes such as common nevi, congenital nevi, and dysplastic nevi. It mentions Familial atypical multiple mole-melanoma (FAMMM) syndrome in the context of dysplastic nevi. The system classifies moles alongside skin tags and warts.

Skin tags are one of the three main conditions identified by the proposed IoT model. The paper distinguishes between 'normal skin tag' and 'cancer skin tag' in the context of differential diagnosis, though the primary classification task groups them with moles and warts.
Warts are identified as a primary target class. The paper defines a wart as an abnormality with intensity ranges above 500. It is classified alongside moles and skin tags using the proposed model.

Melanoma is mentioned in the literature review (Table 1) regarding other studies (Strzelecki et al., Manimurugan et al.) and in the description of dysplastic nevi/FAMMM syndrome. It is not listed as a target class for the classification experiments in this specific paper, which focuses on moles, skin tags, and warts.
Table 3 lists 'Normal' as a target group in the image count comparison, serving as a negative control or baseline class against which moles, skin tags, and warts are compared.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories: moles, skin tags, and warts.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the use of a 'Random Forest (RF) algorithm' for 'forecasting problems' and 'classifying skin tags, moles, and warts'. It mentions 'Periodic classification' for predicting results and reports 'classification accuracy' (87%) and 'total samples rate' for these specific discrete classes. The system identifies these conditions as distinct categories.

datasets_used.name: Existing image dataset (unspecified)
datasets_used.description: A collection of skin images used to identify moles, skin tags, and warts. The paper refers to it as 'existing dataset images' and 'inbuilt database storage' but does not provide a specific public or private dataset name (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000). The images had resolutions ranging from 480 × 640 to 2336 × 3504 pixels.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reports accuracy metrics (e.g., 95.9%, 87%) and correlation performance but does not perform statistical hypothesis testing to compare groups or validate significance.

components: Generalize Approximate Reasoning Base Intelligence Control (GARIC) with regression rules
Boltzmann belief network deep learning technique
architecture_details.description: The system integrates an IoT model with automatic lumen detection and variation regularity. It utilizes a trigonometric algorithm for imaging and analysis, spatial transcriptomics for defining transcriptional patterns, and a Boltzmann belief network for training AI data. A Random Forest classifier is used for the final prediction of moles, skin tags, and warts.

training_details.details: The RF algorithm generates multiple training datasets using the bagging method and makes final decisions based on majority voting. A Boltzmann belief network deep learning technique was used to train data for artificial intelligence (AI). Genome wide association study (GWAS) was utilized to forecast diseases after regularization.

classification_or_output_head.method: Majority voting from multiple decision trees generated via bagging.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Generalize Approximate Reasoning Base Intelligence Control (GARIC)
preprocessing_techniques: Gamma correction (gamma adjustment) to adapt images to the cluster
Pixel extraction in a low pixel range
Pixel intensity analysis for classification (ranges: 390–430 Px for moles, 430–500 Px for skin tags, >500 Px for warts)
conclusions: The proposed Retracing-efficient IoT model, utilizing automatic lumen detection and IoT-based variation regularity, effectively identifies moles, skin tags, and warts.
The system achieves a heightened intensity accuracy of 56.2% and an enhanced low-level total sample accuracy of 95.9% on image groups ranging from 500 to 10,000 images.
Periodic classification on image counts yields a classification accuracy of 87%.

Correlation performance analysis of lumen detection resolution image pixels demonstrates an enhanced correlation performance accuracy of 23.50% on images with resolutions between 480 × 640 and 2336 × 3504 pixels.

The approach contributes to the development of a robust platform for the early detection and diagnosis of skin conditions, addressing the limitations of manual detection which can lead to misclassification due to similar intensity and color levels.
important_remarks: The dataset used is unspecified ('existing dataset images' and 'inbuilt database storage') with no public name or link provided, preventing reproducibility and external validation.

The study claims to use 'spatial transcriptomics' and 'GWAS' for disease forecasting, but the methodology and results focus exclusively on image processing (lumen detection, intensity, edge width) of moles, skin tags, and warts, creating a significant disconnect between claimed molecular methods and actual computational tasks.

The term 'lumen' is defined in the paper as 'the image region that depicts the imaged piece of tissue relating to the camera,' which is a non-standard and potentially confusing definition in dermatology (where lumen typically refers to the inside space of a tubular structure like a blood vessel or colon).

The paper reports 'heightened intensity accuracy' of 56.2% and 'enhanced low-level total sample accuracy' of 95.9%, but the definitions of these metrics are vague and not standard in dermatological AI literature (e.g., standard metrics like sensitivity, specificity, AUC are missing).

The classification accuracy is reported as 87% for image counts ranging from 500 to 10,000, but the specific train/test split, cross-validation strategy, or handling of class imbalance is not detailed.

The paper mentions 'Periodic classification' and using a 'periodic table' to analyze skin diseases, which is methodologically unclear and lacks scientific justification in the context of image classification.
The study relies on a 'digital survey' and 'questionnaire' for feature analysis alongside image data, introducing potential self-reporting bias and confounding variables not addressed in the model.
The paper does not report p-values, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests for the reported accuracy metrics.

The model architecture combines disparate technologies (IoT, Boltzmann belief networks, Random Forest, GARIC) without clear justification for why this specific hybrid approach is superior to standard deep learning models for skin lesion classification.

The paper claims to detect 'cancer skin tags' but does not explicitly include melanoma or other malignant lesions as a target class in the experimental results, limiting its clinical relevance for cancer detection.
